sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: The molecular pathogenesis of ovarian cancer is highly subtype-dependent.

sections.heading: High-grade serous carcinoma
sections.content: High-grade serous carcinoma is characterized by near-universal TP53 mutations (>96%), and frequently exhibits homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) due to germline or somatic BRCA1/2 mutations or other alterations in HR genes (e.g., BRIP1, RAD51C/D). This genomic instability is a key therapeutic target for PARP inhibitors.

sections.heading: Low-grade serous carcinoma
sections.content: In contrast, low-grade serous carcinomas are driven by activating mutations in the MAPK pathway, with BRAF V600E mutations found in approximately 30-50% and KRAS mutations in 20-30% of cases. TP53 mutations are rare in this subtype.

sections.heading: Endometrioid and clear cell carcinomas
sections.content: Endometrioid and clear cell carcinomas are often associated with endometriosis and have distinct molecular profiles. Endometrioid carcinomas frequently harbor CTNNB1 (beta-catenin) mutations, ARID1A mutations, and microsatellite instability (MSI) due to defects in mismatch repair. Clear cell carcinomas commonly show ARID1A mutations and PIK3CA mutations.

sections.heading: Mucinous carcinomas
sections.content: Mucinous carcinomas often have KRAS mutations.

sections.heading: Malignant germ cell tumors
sections.content: Malignant germ cell tumors have distinct pathogenesis, with dysgerminomas associated with overexpression of KIT.

sections.heading: Sex cord-stromal tumors
sections.content: Sex cord-stromal tumors, such as adult granulosa cell tumors, are characterized by a pathognomonic somatic FOXL2 C134W mutation.

sections.heading: Small cell carcinoma of the ovary, hypercalcemic type (SCCOHT)
sections.content: Small cell carcinoma of the ovary, hypercalcemic type (SCCOHT) is defined by bi-allelic loss-of-function mutations in the SMARCA4 (BRG1) gene.
